content.full_text:
i.g
.
Canrorma
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 9,
1994
Honorable Jay Palmquist
Chairperson
Yuba County Board of Supervisors
1818
Sampson St.
Marysville,
CA
95901
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
A—94-264
Dear Mr. Palmquist:
-
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
Are you prohibited from entering into a contract in your
private capacity with the Yuba County Office of Education,
a
governmental agency not under the budgetary or appointive control
of the Board of Supervisors?
CONCLUSION
The conflict-of-interesf provisions of the Act do not
prohibit you from entering into a contract with the Yuba County
Office of Education.
The Act generally prohibits a public official who has a
material financial interest in a decision from attempting to use
his or her official position to influence that decision.
However,
a public official can represent his or her private interests
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-264
Page
2
before an agency not under the budgetary or appointive control of
the official’s own agency, provided he or she does not act or
purport to act on behalf of his or her own agency.
FACTS
You are a member of the Yuba County Board of Supervisors.
You own commercial property which is available for leasing of
office space.
The Yuba County Office of Education conducted a pre-bid
workshop for persons interested in responding to a Request For
Quote for the leasing of approximately 5,600 to 6,000 square feet
of office space.
The leased property will house the Yuba County
Office of Education, Job Development and Training Department, the
Yuba County Service provider for the Federal Job Training
Partnership Act.
As a private citizen you submitted a sealed quote.
All
sealed quotes were publicly opened and read aloud on May 9,
1994,
by the Office of Education.
On June 10,
1994,
you received a
letter from the Superintendent of Yuba County Schools informing
you that your quote was one of the final two that were being
considered.
After touring the facility and responding to
pertinent questions concerning parking space, potential expansion,
needed alterations,
and the final cost per square foot, the
Superintendent offered to enter into a lease agreement with you.
As a member of the Yuba County Board of Supervisors,
you are
not involved in any decisionmaking process concerning the leasing
of property by the Yuba County Office of Education.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in whiph the official has a financial
interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision that
will have a foreseeable and material financial effect, different
from the effect on the general public,
on the official or on the
official’s immediate family or on the following:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
** **
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a)
and (d).


Our File No. A-94-264
Page
3
As a member of the Yuba County Board of Supervisors you are a
public official.
(Section 82048.)
Therefore,
you are
disqualified from participating in any decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your
business interests as noted above.
(Sections 82005,
87100,
and
87103.)
Disqualification generally means that an official cannot
make, participate in making,
or otherwise try to influence a
decision when acting within the authority of his or her position.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700; Regulation 18700.1; Levinger
Advice Letter,
No.
1—88—328; West Advice Letter,
No. A-88-413.)
Regulation 18700.1 provides that even conduct in an
official’s private capacity may be characterized as using his or
her official position to influence a decision.
Regulation 18700.1 provides in relevant part:
(a)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include,
but are not
limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client,
or
customer.
****
(c)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or befoe an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the officiàl is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer,
employee or
consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.
Regulation 18700.1(a)
and (c).
(Emphasis added.)
According to your facts and our telephone conversation of
July 28,
1994, the contract in question will not concern the board
of supervisors or any agency subject to the board of supervisor’s
budgetary or appointive control.
However,
you are concerned about
the potential conflicts which may arise in your public capacity


Our File No. A-94-264
Page
4
resulting from entering into a contract with another governmental
agency.
Pursuant to Regulation 18700.1(c), as long as you do not act
or purport to represent the board of supervisors, you are not
restricted by the Political Reform Act from entering into a
contract with the Board of Education or from entering into
communications with the contracting agency relating to. the
contract.
However, please note that the governmental agency will become
a potentially disqualifying source ofincome to you if you receive
$250 or more from that entity.
(Section 87103(c).)
Thus, if
governmental decisions arise which may affect the Yuba County
Board of Education,
you should seek additional guidance from us.
In addition, please note that ouradvice is limited to the
Political Reform Act.
You may want to seek further guidance from
your county counsel or the Attorney General’s Office regarding
other provisions of the law such as Government Code Section 1090.
I trust this letter has provided you with guidance regarding
the duties and responsibilities.
If you have any further
questions regarding these matters, please feel free to contact me
at
(916)
322_590l.2
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
2
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.
